• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

Did Rexburg run out of snow?

Winter 2025-2026 was very peculiar. Many people in Rexburg Idaho made the warmer weather a topic for small talk. Many were disappointed in the lack of snow. The locals were very adamant that this is quite rare for Rexburg, sometimes dubbed as “The Iceburg.” This analysis seeks to investigate whether this year’s winter temperatures were significantly different from other year’s winters, or if the peculiarity is over-exaggerated.

rexburg <- get_GSOD(
  years   = 2000:2025,
  station = "726818-94194")
rexburg <- rexburg %>% 
  select(NAME, YEAR, MONTH, DAY, YDAY, TEMP) %>% 
  filter(YEAR <2025)

rexburg2026 <- read.csv("../../Data/rexburg2026.csv")

rexburg2026 <- rexburg2026 %>% 
  select(NAME = Station_name, DATE, TEMP = temperature) %>%
  mutate(
    DATE = ymd_hms(DATE),
    YEAR = year(DATE),
    MONTH = month(DATE),
    DAY = day(DATE),
    YDAY = yday(DATE)
  ) %>%
  group_by(NAME, YEAR, MONTH, DAY, YDAY) %>%
  summarise(
    TEMP = mean(TEMP, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

rexburg2025 <- read.csv("../../Data/rexburg_2025.csv") %>% 
  select(NAME = Station_name, DATE, TEMP= temperature) %>% 
  mutate(
    DATE = ymd_hms(DATE),
    YEAR = year(DATE),
    MONTH = month(DATE),
    DAY = day(DATE),
    YDAY =yday(DATE)
  ) %>% 
  group_by(NAME, YEAR, MONTH, DAY, YDAY) %>% 
  summarise(
    TEMP = mean(TEMP, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

rdf <- bind_rows(rexburg,rexburg2025,rexburg2026)

rdf$TEMP <- (rdf$TEMP * 9/5) + 32
rdf <- rdf %>%
  filter(MONTH %in% c(11, 12, 1, 2, 3)) %>%
  mutate(
    recentwinter = case_when(
      YEAR == 2025 & MONTH %in% c(11, 12) ~ 1,
      YEAR == 2026 & MONTH %in% c(1, 2, 3) ~ 1,
      TRUE ~ 0
    )
  )

rdf <- rdf %>% 
  mutate(
    winteryear = case_when(
      MONTH %in% c(11, 12) ~ YEAR,
      MONTH %in% c(1, 2, 3) ~ YEAR - 1
    )
  ) %>%
  arrange(winteryear, YEAR, MONTH, DAY) %>%
  group_by(winteryear) %>%
  mutate(
    wdays = row_number()
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>% 
  filter(wdays <118)

The Data

Hide Table

This data is taken from weather information taken from the Rexburg Madison County Airport. It shows data spanning 25 years. This specific dataset has been filtered to only show temperature observations during the winter season. Due to dataset constraints for the most recent winter, the winter season has been constrained to span from November 1 - February 25 for each year.

Show Table

datatable(rdf)

The Model

A multiple regression model was applied to the data to fit 2 regression lines, one for the recent winter and another for the average of all previous winters. The model is as follows:

\[\underbrace{Y_i}_{\text{temperature}} = \overbrace{\beta_0 + \beta_1 \underbrace{X_{i1}}_{\text{Previous Winter (days)}}}^{\text{Previous Winter}} + \overbrace{\beta_2 \underbrace{X_{i2}}_{\text{1 if recent winter}} + \beta_3 \underbrace{X_{i1} X_{i2}}_{\text{Interaction}}}^{\text{Recent Winter Adjustments to Line}} + \epsilon_i\]

Hypothesis Tests

Test for Equal y-Intercepts

\[ H_0: \beta_2 = 0 \quad \text{(Equal average temperature at start of season)} \\ H_a: \beta_2 \neq 0 \quad \text{(Non-equal average temperature at start of season)}\]

Test for Equal Slopes

\[ H_0: \beta_3 = 0 \quad \text{( The recent winter has the same average temperature trend as previous winters.)} \\ H_a: \beta_3 \neq 0 \quad \text{(The recent winter has a different temperature trend than previous winters.)} \]

The Results

The scatter plot and regression lines below model the temperature in Rexburg as the winter season progresses. Results show that the recent winter was around 8.946 degrees warmer compared to previous winters (P-value = 1.638e-06). The slopes of the prior winters aren’t significantly different from each other as the p-value of the interaction is \(> \alpha = 0.05\) at (P-value = 0.8117).

lm.rwinter <- lm(TEMP ~ wdays + as.factor(recentwinter) + wdays:as.factor(recentwinter), data = rdf)
plot(TEMP ~ wdays, data = rdf,
     col = c("skyblue", "orange")[as.factor(rdf$recentwinter)],
     pch = 21, bg = "gray83",
     yaxt = "n",
     xlab = "Days of Winter",
     ylab = "Temperature in °F"
    )
    title(main = "Recent Winter ~9°F Warmer Compared to Previous Rexburg Winters")
    mtext("Data from Year January 1 2000 - February 25 2026")

axis(2, at = seq(floor(min(rdf$TEMP, na.rm = TRUE)),
                 ceiling(max(rdf$TEMP, na.rm = TRUE)),
                 by = 2))

abline(h = 32, col = "navy", lty = 3, lwd = 2)
text(x = 100,
     y = 32,
     labels = "Freezing Point",
     col = "navy",
     pos = 3,
     font = 2)
points(x = 69, y = 32, pch = 19, col = "navy", cex = 1.5)
text(x = 69, y = 34, labels = "January 8 2026", col = "navy")

b <- coef(lm.rwinter)

curve(b[1] + b[2]*x, col="navy", lwd=2, add=TRUE)  #baseline (in blue)
curve((b[1] + b[3]) + (b[2] + b[4])*x, col="orange", lwd=2, add=TRUE) #changed line (in orange)

legend("topright",
       legend = c("Previous Winters", "Recent Winter"),
       col = c("navy", "orange"),
       lwd = 2,
       bty = "n")

summary(lm.rwinter) %>% 
  pander(title = "Regression Summary output for Temperature Across Winter Season")
  Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept) 30.76 0.3679 83.63 0
wdays -0.1061 0.005532 -19.19 2.284e-77
as.factor(recentwinter)1 8.946 1.862 4.803 1.638e-06
wdays:as.factor(recentwinter)1 -0.006534 0.02742 -0.2383 0.8117
Fitting linear model: TEMP ~ wdays + as.factor(recentwinter) + wdays:as.factor(recentwinter)
Observations Residual Std. Error \(R^2\) Adjusted \(R^2\)
2925 9.811 0.1381 0.1372

Linear Regression Table Analysis

From this table we can see 4 estimated model coefficients, to be able to answer our hypothesis question, we need to look at the:

“as.factor(recentwinter)” coefficient, also known as the intercept value difference of the recent winter and previous winter, which is showing to be 8.946℉ higher than the previous winter. Because the probability of getting a result as extreme as 8.94℉ is at (P-value = 1.638e-06) we reject the null hypothesis that the average temperature of this winter at the start of the season is statistically equal to the average temperature at the start of the season for other winters.

“wdays:as.factor(recentwinter)” coefficient, also known as the difference of the slope of the previous winters and the recent winter. The estimate is showing a -0.006534 difference and a p-value of (p-value = 0.8117). Because our \(\alpha = 0.05\), we fail to reject the null hypothesis, thus indicating that the rate of temperature drop is the same as previous winters.

In Conclusion

The most recent winter is shown to be about 8.85°F warmer than previous winters overall. Temperatures in previous winters dropped by about 0.106°F per day, while temperatures in the recent winter dropped by about 0.113°F per day. Because the difference in slope is not shown to be statistically significant (p = 0.8117), this suggests the recent winter followed the same cooling pattern as earlier winters, but at a consistently higher temperature level. A ~9℉ difference is quite a lot, as displayed earlier in the graph, snow typically doesn’t form until the temperature goes below 32℉ (freezing point). This affects snow sports, and even the water level in water reservoirs as snow acts as a slow release water source that melts in the spring.

Checking Assumptions

The tests below show that the results of this analysis may be questionable. The residuals vs fitted plot show signs of non linearity and show that the variance of the error terms may not be constant. The qqPlot also has a lot of values that are outside of the line signifying that the error terms may not be normally distributed. The error terms seem to be independent as shown in the residuals vs order plot, but overall, we have to take the results of the analysis with a grain of salt

par(mfrow=c(1,3))
plot(lm.rwinter, which=1)
qqPlot(lm.rwinter$residuals)
## [1] 1549 2548
mtext(side=3,text="Q-Q Plot of Residuals")
plot(lm.rwinter$residuals, type="b")
mtext(side=3, text="Residuals vs. Order")
